one_line_takeaway: Public LLMs can be prompted without fine-tuning to mass-produce cybersecurity-look-alike text that pushes a state-of-the-art CTI classifier to 97% false-positive rate and, via the retraining feedback loop, collapses its F1 to 0.57 - showing CTI pipelines need early-stage input verification, not just robust models.
key_finding: Specialized CTI classifiers are far more fragile than the LLM classifiers: the Dionisio et al. CNN reached 97% FPR on optimized adversarial Twitter text (up from 87% on un-optimized), versus 75% for ChatGPT-4o and 68% for GPT-5-mini. Under threshold-independent evaluation the CNN's ROC AUC collapsed to 0.523 (near random) with average precision 0.533, confirming it keys on shallow lexical/structural cues (CVE-like tokens, CTI sentence shape) rather than semantics. The attacks chain: evasion is a strict prerequisite because only positively-classified text propagates to monitoring/retraining. Progressive poisoning with the 9402 FaN drove F1 from 0.9349 (round 1) to 0.5730 (round 7) and recall from 0.93 to 0.49, with false negatives rising 734->5537. Paraphrase-based FaP (88% passed as TP) slightly out-deceived rule-based FaP (82%). Conclusion: CTI pipelines need an early-stage fact-checking/verification component before ingestion.
core_contribution: The paper delivers a system-level (pipeline-wide) study of how adversarially generated text degrades automated text-based Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) extraction, rather than yet another single-model attack. Its own five contributions are: (1) an integrated reference CTI pipeline abstracting five stages seen across prior systems (data collection, AI-based analysis, monitoring/validation, threat scoring, actionability); (2) an attention-based fake-text-generation methodology that uses SecureBERT+LSTM+attention to find the top-3 most important cybersecurity tokens and then prompts a public LLM (ChatGPT-4o / GPT-5-mini, no fine-tuning) to rewrite the SURROUNDING tokens so the output keeps CTI-like surface structure while stripping real security semantics (producing 'Fake true Negatives', FaN); (3) a system-level analysis showing structural weaknesses emerge from interactions between pipeline stages; (4) a characterization of evasion, flooding, and poisoning threats against the pipeline; and (5) an empirical demonstration that these three attacks are causally chained (evasion is the prerequisite that enables downstream flooding and poisoning). Note: the multi-step optimization frameworks EaTVul, TextJuggler, TextGuise, Grover, ARGH, and Ren et al. appear only in Section 8 Related Work / Table 7 as comparison points and are NOT this paper's own methods; the authors explicitly position their generation-based, semantics-discarding approach against those constrained-perturbation, model-level baselines.
threat_model: Black-box adversary against an automated OSINT-fed CTI pipeline. Formalized knowledge tuple k=(D,X,f,L,w): the attacker cannot access training data D, features X, algorithm f, objective L, or trained weights w, but can approximate D via public OSINT (e.g. CVE) and build a substitute model, interacting only through input/output. Capability is tiered: evasion = low-capability (inference-time, public tools, no model internals), flooding = mid-capability (sustained volume injection into ingestion), poisoning = high-capability (persistent, optimized, diverse FaN generation that must pass human analysts and reach the retraining set). Attacker goal is to inject fake cybersecurity-like text into open sources (Twitter/X, forums, blogs, dark web) to inflate False Positives, flood/DoS the SOC dashboard, and eventually corrupt the model's decision boundary via the feedback/retraining loop.
limitations: Authors' own stated limits: (1) the whole evaluation assumes a single unified pipeline architecture centered on a BINARY classifier; ASR results may not generalize to multi-class/open-set or differently-architected pipelines. (2) Poisoning assumes some fakes reach the model's training data via the feedback loop, which fails if the model is outsourced or trained on curated data. (3) Heavy reliance on human analysts, who make fatigue/experience-dependent judgment errors. (4) Reuse of the SAME analysts for FaN quality-filtering and for pipeline alarm-validation introduces bias (they knew texts were fake) and was a resource-driven choice. (5) Fact-checking, the proposed defense, is acknowledged insufficient against paraphrased flooding inputs that preserve semantic plausibility. (6) Short (<=256 char) tweets constrain attacker deception (worst-case for attacker); long-text generation coherence is a noted open challenge. Poisoning-via-flooding (FaP into training set) was described but NOT experimentally demonstrated.

datasets_used: Twitter/X CTI corpus (Dionisio et al. [46]): 31,281 tweets, 11,074 (35.4%) security-related, 20,207 (64.6%) non-security; NER-annotated; collected 2016/2017/2018
AnnoCTR [47]: 400 CTI reports (2020-2024), 2,000 sampled segments = 1,500 short (100-256 chars) + 500 longer (256-1000 chars)
quantitative_results: Evasion FPR on Twitter (optimized): Dionisio et al. CNN 97% (87% un-optimized); ChatGPT-4o 75.0%+/-1.7 (51% un-optimized); GPT-5-mini 68.0%+/-1.2 (51% un-optimized)
Evasion FPR on full AnnoCTR (optimized): CNN 97%; ChatGPT-4o 81.0%+/-1.7; GPT-5-mini 74.0%+/-2.5
Evasion FPR on longer AnnoCTR texts (optimized): CNN 96%; ChatGPT-4o 76.3%+/-1.5; GPT-5-mini 69.3%+/-1.5
CNN under evasion: ROC AUC = 0.523 (near-random), average precision = 0.533, on a mixed set of 11,074 real + 5,000 non-security + 5,000 FaN texts
Human filtering: 1,340 of 11,074 FaN rejected -> 9,734 retained; mean cosine similarity of FaN vs real tweets = 0.62 (most scores 0.5-0.8; Shapiro-Wilk p=3.2e-6)
Flooding on CNN: 85% of FaN misclassified as FP (Table 5: 11,074 FaN -> 9,402 FP, FPR 0.97); paraphrased FaP 88% TPR; rule-based FaP 82% TPR (4,282 FN)

Poisoning of CNN over 7 rounds injecting 9,402 cumulative FaN: F1 0.9349 -> 0.5730; recall 0.93 -> 0.49; precision 0.94 -> 0.69; FN 734 -> 5,537; FP 638 -> 2,373 (stays >0.90 F1 until ~4,500 poisoned samples)
Gradient KDE (Table 4): fake vs real distributions nearly identical (Twitter KL divergence 0.05, cosine distance 0.85, Wasserstein 0.0012), indicating adversarial gradients closely imitate real text
baselines_compared: ChatGPT-4o vs GPT-5-mini vs Dionisio et al. [21] CNN classifier (the specialized model is the most vulnerable target)
Un-optimized vs 5-iteration prompt-optimized adversarial text (FPR gains, e.g. CNN 87%->97%, GPT-4o 51%->75%)
Paraphrase-based FaP (GPT-3.5-Turbo, 88% TPR) vs rule-based entity-substitution FaP (82% TPR) for flooding
LLaMA-3 8B/70B (rejected as generators for poor diversity/realism) vs ChatGPT-4o/5-mini

Related-work frameworks in Table 7 (NOT run by the authors): Ren et al. [53], EaTVul, TextJuggler, TextGuise - all single-model, semantics-preserving evasion (ASR 80-100%) with no reported pipeline/F1 degradation, contrasted against this work's pipeline-level F1 drop to 0.57
